stem: Why does gadolinium-enhanced blood appear bright on a T1-weighted MR angiographic acquisition?

options.text: Gadolinium eliminates hydrogen precession
Gadolinium lengthens blood T1 beyond stationary tissue
Gadolinium shortens blood T1 so longitudinal magnetization recovers rapidly
Gadolinium creates a velocity-dependent phase shift without RF

explanation: A gadolinium chelate increases longitudinal relaxation rate, allowing blood to generate strong signal during a rapid T1-weighted gradient echo. This mechanism is distinct from TOF inflow enhancement.

stem: Compared with TOF, what is an advantage of contrast-enhanced MRA?

options.text: It requires no attention to acquisition timing
It provides no venous signal under any circumstance
It quantitatively measures velocity from phase alone
Vessel signal is less dependent on flow direction and repeated-pulse saturation

explanation: Contrast shortens blood T1 throughout the imaged volume, so slow or in-plane flow can remain bright. The acquisition still requires careful timing to capture the desired vascular phase.

stem: What is the main consequence of starting an arterial contrast-enhanced MRA acquisition too late?

options.text: Venous enhancement may contaminate the arterial image
The main magnet loses superconductivity
All gadolinium becomes invisible before reaching vessels
Phase-encoding gradients reverse blood flow

explanation: After the arterial peak, contrast returns through the venous system and reduces arterial-to-venous separation. Timing central k-space near peak arterial enhancement is especially important for contrast.

stem: What is removed when a precontrast mask is subtracted from a postcontrast angiographic dataset?

options.text: The contrast-enhanced vessel signal
Much of the stationary background tissue signal
All motion between the two acquisitions
The receiver noise from both acquisitions

explanation: Background anatomy appears in both datasets and largely cancels, while newly enhanced vessels remain. Patient motion between mask and contrast images can instead cause subtraction artifact.

stem: Which patient factor warrants particular caution when selecting a gadolinium-based contrast agent?

options.text: A normal glomerular filtration rate
Remote uncomplicated radiography
Severe renal dysfunction
A low phase-encoding matrix

explanation: Certain gadolinium agents have been associated with nephrogenic systemic fibrosis in severe renal impairment, so renal status and agent choice matter. Modern practice also considers prior reactions and the need for repeated exposure.
